title: Dolmades
subtitle: Rice-stuffed vine leaves with lemon and herbs
excerpt: Vine leaves rolled around a filling of rice, herbs, and lemon, then simmered until tender. Serve warm with a squeeze of lemon, or at room temperature as part of a mezze spread.
sourceNotes: Classic Greek and Middle Eastern preparation documented in Claudia Roden, A Book of Middle Eastern Food (1968) and Tessa Kiros, Falling Cloudberries (2004). The lemon-and-herb rice filling is common to Greek, Turkish, and Lebanese variants; the ratio of oil to rice is the key variable across traditions.

content.text: Dolmades take time to roll, and that time is the point. The work is repetitive in the good way, the kind that forces a slow afternoon. Each leaf goes down flat, filling placed at the base, sides folded in, then rolled firmly away from you. Once you have the motion, it comes quickly.

The filling here is vegetarian: long-grain rice with dill, parsley, spring onion, lemon zest, and olive oil. The rice absorbs the cooking liquid and the leaf's tannic note as it steams inside. Serve them warm with extra lemon, or let them cool to room temperature and they become part of a

Scalability matters here: the rolling method depends on having enough leaves to line the bottom of the pot (which protects the dolmades from scorching) and enough liquid to create steam. This recipe is built for a specific pot size. Halving it works only if you have a smaller pot.

items.name: Vine leaves
items.prepNote: preserved in brine, rinsed and patted dry

items.name: Lemon
items.prepNote: zest of both, juice of both

items.name: Vegetable stock
items.prepNote: or water with a pinch of salt

content.text: You need a large, heavy-based saucepan deep enough to hold the dolmades in a tight single layer. A plate or heatproof lid slightly smaller than the pan's interior is useful for weighing them down during cooking.

Rinse the rice in cold water until the water runs mostly clear. Drain well. Combine in a bowl with the spring onion, parsley, dill, and mint if using. Add the lemon zest, 60 ml of the olive oil, salt, and pepper. Mix thoroughly. The rice goes in uncooked. It will steam in the vine leaves.

Lay a vine leaf flat, smooth side down, stalk end towards you. Snip off the stalk if it's stiff. Place a heaped teaspoon of filling near the base of the leaf. Fold the bottom sides of the leaf up and over the filling, then fold in the left and right sides, and roll away from you into a firm cylinder. Don't roll too tightly, the rice needs room to expand. Repeat with remaining leaves and filling.

Line the bottom of the pan with 5 or 6 leftover or torn vine leaves (this stops the dolmades scorching). Pack the dolmades seam side down in a tight single layer. Mix the lemon juice, remaining 20 ml of olive oil, and the stock together and pour over. Place a heatproof plate directly on top of the dolmades to keep them from unrolling. Cover the pan and bring to a gentle simmer over medium heat, then reduce to low. Cook for 45 to 50 minutes until the rice is tender when a dolma is cut open. Check once halfway through and add a little water if the pan looks dry.

Remove from heat and leave with the lid on for 10 minutes. Lift gently onto a serving plate. Pour over any remaining cooking juices. Serve warm or at room temperature with lemon wedges and plain Greek yoghurt or tzatziki alongside.

items.fix: Add another 100 ml of hot water, replace the weighted plate, and continue cooking for 15 to 20 minutes more
items.cause: Not enough liquid or heat too low; the steam wasn't sufficient to cook the rice through
items.symptom: Rice is still hard after 50 minutes

items.fix: Pack them more snugly next time; the tight packing is what keeps them rolled, not the fold alone
items.cause: Rolled too loosely or not packed tightly enough in the pot

items.fix: Always line the base with spare leaves; cook over the lowest possible simmer
items.cause: No vine-leaf lining at the bottom, or heat too high

items.fix: Season the filling assertively. It should taste slightly over-salted raw, as the vine leaves absorb some salt during cooking
items.cause: Under-seasoned rice mixture; fresh herbs lose intensity during cooking

content.text: Dolmades appear across the entire eastern Mediterranean and into the Caucasus, Greek, Turkish, Lebanese, Iranian, Armenian versions exist, each with regional variations in filling and seasoning. The Greek vegetarian version with rice, lemon, and herbs is the one most often served cold as part of a mezze. Meat-filled versions (with lamb mince, onion, and tomato) are more often served warm as a main course. Both trace back to the Ottoman kitchen, where stuffed vegetables were a central technique.
